question.text: In the context of derivative risks, what is 'leverage risk'?

answer.text: The risk that arises because derivatives require only a fraction of the contract value as margin, amplifying both gains and losses relative to the capital deployed
The risk of a company taking too much debt on its balance sheet
The risk that the exchange will increase margin requirements during volatile periods
The risk that a trader cannot find a counterparty for their derivative position

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Derivatives are leveraged instruments — an equity futures position worth Rs 10 lakhs may require only Rs 1-2 lakhs as margin. This leverage amplifies returns: a 10% move in the underlying creates a 50-100% change in the margin capital. Both gains and losses are magnified. **The Trap:** While margin increases do create risk, leverage risk is fundamentally about the amplification effect. Liquidity risk (finding counterparties) is a separate concept. **Cross-Exam Context:** Leverage exists in all derivative markets — equity F&O (Series VIII), currency futures (Series I), IR futures (Series IV), and commodity futures (Series XVI). Series XIII compares leverage across markets. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Leverage is a double-edged sword — always calculate maximum loss scenarios before taking derivative positions. Never risk more capital than you can afford to lose.
